#37 How an idiot hikes in Japan (part 2)

May 18, 2024 1 hour 46 minutes

All transcripts are machine generated.

So because I lost my passport in 2019, basically what happened is I always kept my passport in my bag and just left it behind. Suppose that you're supposed to have it on you in case a cop stops you, but I figured, you know, if that did happen, I could just, if the cop really wanted to see my passport bad enough, I could take him to the Airbnb, just show him my passport. I didn't want to have it on me. I figured it was safer just to keep it in my bag. But then by Naka Itabashi, I was staying in a communal place with other people, which was not something I often did then, and still sort of strive not to now.

So I did just leave my bag in the Airbnb generally because it's like, I would ever. What's in there that matters? Who gives a shit? Worst case scenario, someone stole my laptop, I'll just buy another one at that point too. I actually have a good laptop now, which is a little bit of a downside for traveling. It's like, oh no, I have an expensive laptop now. Back then my laptop was shit. So I did not care. But I didn't want to leave the passport because I thought maybe that's the one thing that someone might actually take that actually could be useful and that I would be fucked without.

So I had my passport with me, but it was summer and it was really hot. So I just had it in the inside 30:00 pocket of my hoodie. And it wasn't nowadays all of my, all my layers have pockets with zippers. It's very handy to have the zipper. You can put something in your pocket and it ain't coming out. Even if it's something as little as just a coffee can from a drink machine that you just have to keep on you till you find a garbage, you know. It's just a lot easier with a zipper. But I didn't have a zipper on my hoodie pocket back then.

So I just put it in this interior pocket, but I wasn't wearing the hoodie most of the time because it was too hot out. So I just had it around my waist or over my shoulder or whatever. And at some point the passport just fell out. I don't know when, I don't know where. It was just days later. I was like looking through all my stuff. And it's like there's just, there's just no passport here. It's just gone.

And I am quite sure that I just lost it, like it didn't get stolen. Because the people at the passport office, they were actually quite surprised that it hadn't been returned. Because just again with like the basic sort of honor system of Japan that have already seen evidence of like many times all over the place in Japan from little vegetable stands all the way up to big department stores, the level of security is so much lower than North America. You just trust that you're not going to skirt the system and you're not going to try to fuck with things, you're not going to steal stuff. And Japanese people are pretty famous for like any time a wallet gets returned to a police station that the money is still in it and stuff like that. Where North America, maybe someone will return your wallet, maybe. But the money will certainly be gone.

I actually found an abandoned wallet, like a purse in my hometown. Somebody had just scattered all the shit. But it was like an ID and things that maybe were important. And of course anything valuable, money credit cards, those were gone. But I gathered up all the stuff, put it back in the purse, and I could see the address because it was ID and things, drivers licenses and whatnot. And the house was like a 40 minute walk away. So I just went and put it in their mailbox. I hope it helped. But that's the best you're going to get in Canada, you know. Whereas in Japan, it was pretty much like taken as a given that if someone had found my passport, they would just return it. They would return it to the police station and no one did.

So yeah, I presume it just fell in a gutter or something somewhere. I wanted my many weird walks right in the woods or something. And it was gone. There's an anime too, but don't watch the anime. The anime has CG cars and silly Eurobeat music. Where the manga is kind of austere and cool. And arguably kind of meditative to read because it is hundreds and hundreds of very short chapters. I think it was a weekly comic. So every week a new chapter would come out. So it was not uncommon. Oh, here's the path up into the woods. That looks cool, but I'm afraid not. Not today. It's still sunny out, but that would be the most insane thing I could ever do. But yeah, there'll be chapters. Usually there's a lot of either people with a lot of exposition explaining the current state of the race or just sometimes captions. But sometimes there's chapters that are just driving. Just nothing happens except the two racers focusing on the road and taking turns. But yeah, strangely meditative to read because each race takes so long and so little happens in any given chapter. But it's also interesting like how does it keep upping the ante? You know, like once you've used one secret racing trick, you can't really use it again. Now you got to come up with a new secret racing trick. So one of the secret racing tricks, what is these gutters? The idea of the story is that the main character, his car, is not actually as good as everyone else's car. But he's been secretly trained by his dad during tofu delivery runs to drive this sports car. So he didn't even realize it was a sports car. He didn't realize that, you know, he just thought it was his dad being lazy. Like, a dad just doesn't want to get up before I am to go deliver tofu up in the mountains. 1:25:00 But that gave him this head start of many years over everyone else where he was driving before he should have been allowed to drive. So his car is not as fast as everyone else's car, but his technique is so good that he keeps finding ways to eke out victories. And one of them are these gutters that you can really take a turn at a fairly severe rate of speed. Because if you watch like people do drifting in real life, it's weirdly slow. Drifting is like a weird, I don't know how sensible of a technique it really is in reality because you lose so much speed when you're sliding around turns. So he slid at a much too high rate of speed that should have just sent him Karin in off the road. But he got his wheel kind of wedged inside one of these gutters. That's probably wildly unrealistic. I doubt that that's even possible. But in the comic they're like, oh, shit, that's a crazy technique. First off, who would ever think to do that? And second off, that fucks up your axles so bad and ruins your tires. Like, you're going to pay for that later in the race. And it's such a perfect example of what is exciting about that comic. pantomime. Man, I'll take that. I'll take a pantomime any day. But the whole place was spotless, just spick and span, one of the nicest 7-Elevens ever. I'm just watching people come and go. It's so strange too with just people driving on the other side of the road. So the wheel is on the other side of the car. So a car pulled into the parking lot. But it's just that that that brought back weird memories where that happens a lot in Japan where you get these throwback memories largely based on smoking. But every time you see a cigarette machine or you see a smoking room, I've been seeing more and more of those there all over the place when you start searching for them, the smoking rooms. It feels like the 80s. It's like, oh yeah, back in the time when everybody smoked. And then even just the way people dress. I've heard it described as like a throwback all the way to the 50s. Like it's quite modest around here. It's just skirts mostly. You know, maybe a mini skirt if you're really going out on the town. A lot of shawls and sweaters. It's like almost like grandma chic. But it's kind of cool. And yeah, that just reminded me that leaving your kids in the car parked car while you go do something. That's one of those things that goes in waves where in my case, it would be quite short stays. It's like hey, just wait here. And then a parent would go do whatever they had to do. But it might be like the grocery store or something. It would be like, I remember being left in a car with my brother for 10 or 20 minutes, probably. And now in the current era, you would just never do that. You know, you'd, because I also when I was a kid, it's like, all right, I wouldn't even say I'm leaving. I wouldn't tell anybody. Just hop on a bicycle and go drive to a different neighborhood and fuck around with my friends. And no one ever knew what I was up to or what I was doing. And that's just not, that's not on anymore. Which is one of those things. It's probably more sensible that people are a little more aware of what the heck their kids are doing. But it's kind of a neat nostalgic throwback to that feeling. But it was so much more extreme in my mom's age where she said like her dad would go to like the Legion Hall and go in and get drunk with his friends and leave whatever gaggle of kids he had with him that day because she has 12 brothers and sisters just in the car for hours.
